13	# The Long Reign—Jaehaerys and Alysanne: Policy, Progeny, and Pain
14	
15	The Long Reign
16	
17	Jachaerys and Alysanne: Policy, Progeny, and Pain n the seventh day of the 59th year after Aegon’s Conquest, a battered ship came limping up the Whispering Sound to the port of Oldtown. Her sails were patched and ragged and salt-stained, her paint faded and flaking, the banner streaming from her mast so sun-bleached as to be unrecognizable. Not until she was tied up at dock was she finally recognized in her sorry state. She was the Lady Meredith, last seen departing
18	
19	Oldtown almost three years earlier to cross the Sunset Sea.
20	
21	As her crew began to disembark, throngs of merchants, porters, whores, seamen, and thieves gaped in shock. Nine of every ten men coming ashore were black or brown. Ripples of excitement ran up and down the docks. Had the Lady Meredith indeed crossed the Sunset Sea? Were the peoples of the fabled lands of the far west all dark-skinned as Summer Islanders?
22	
23	Only when Ser Eustace Hightower himself emerged did the whispers die. Lord Donnel’s grandson was gaunt and sun-burned, with lines on his face that had not been there when he sailed. A handful of Oldtown men were with him, all that remained of his original crew. One of his grandsire’s customs officers met him on the dock and a quick exchange ensued. The Lady Meredith's crew did not simply look like Summer Islanders; they were Summer Islanders, hired on in Sothoryos (“at ruinous wages,” Ser Eustace complained) to replace the men he'd lost. He would require porters, the captain said. His holds were bulging with rich cargo...but not from lands beyond the Sunset Sea. “That was a dream,” he said. Soon enough Lord Donnel’s knights turned up, with orders to escort him to the Hightower. There, in his grandsire's high hall with a cup of wine in hand, Ser Eustace Hightower told his tale. Lord Donnel’s scribes
24	
25	scribbled as he spoke, and within days the story had spread all over Westeros, by messenger, bard, and raven.
26	
27	The voyage had begun as well as he could have hoped, Ser Eustace said. Once beyond the Arbor, Lady Westhill had steered her Sun Chaser south by southwest, seeking warmer waters and fair winds, and the Lady Meredith and Autumn Moon had followed. The big Braavosi ship was very fast when the wind was in her sails, and the Hightowers had difficulties keeping pace. “The Seven were smiling on us, at the start. We had the sun by day and the moon by night, and as sweet a wind as man or maid could hope for. We were not entirely alone. We glimpsed fisherfolk from time to time, and once a great dark ship that could only have been a whaler out of Ib. And fish, so many fish...some dolphins swam beside us, as if they had never seen a ship before. We all
28	
29	thought that we were blessed.”
30	
31	Twelve days of smooth sailing out of Westeros, the Sun Chaser and her two companions were as far south as the Summer Islands, according to their best calculations, and farther west than any ship had sailed before... or any ship that had returned to tell of it, at least. On the Lady Meredith and Autumn Moon, casks of Arbor
32	
33	gold were breached to toast the accomplishment; on Sun Chaser, the sailors drank a spiced honey wine from Lannisport. And if any man of them was disquieted that they had not seen a bird for the past four days, he held
34	
35	his tongue.
36	
37	The gods hate man’s arrogance, the septons teach us, and The Seven-Pointed Star says that pride goes before a fall. It may well be that Alys Westhill and the Hightowers celebrated too loudly and too early, there in the ocean deeps, for soon after that the grand voyage began to go badly wrong. “We lost the wind first,” Ser Eustace told his grandfather's court. “For almost a fortnight there was not so much as a breeze, and the ships moved only so far as we could tow them. It was discovered that a dozen casks of meat on Autumn Moon were crawling with maggots. A small enough thing by itself, but an ill omen. The wind finally returned one day near sunset, when the sky turned red as blood, but the look of it set men to muttering. | told them it boded well for
38	
39	us, but | lied. Before morning the stars were gone and the wind began to howl, and then the ocean rose.”
40	
41	That was the first storm, Ser Eustace said. Another followed two days later, and then a third, each worse than the one before. “The waves rose higher than our masts, and there was thunder all around, and lightning such as | had never seen before, great cracking bolts that burned the eyes. One struck the Autumn Moon and split her mast from the crow's nest down to the deck. In the midst of all that madness, one of my hands screamed that he had seen arms rising from the water, the last thing any captain needs to hear. We had lost all
42	
43	sight of Sun Chaser by then, all that remained was my lady and the Moon. The sea was washing over our decks with every rise and fall, and men were being swept over the side, clinging uselessly to lines. | saw the Autumn Moon founder with my own eyes. One moment she was there, broken and burning, but there. Then a wave rose up and swallowed her and | blinked and she was gone, quick as that. That was all it was, a wave, a monster of a
44	
45	wave, but all my men were screaming ‘Kraken, kraken!’ and not a word | said would ever disabuse them.
46	
47	“| will never know how we survived that night, but we did. The next morning the sea was calm again, the sun was shining, and the water was so blue and innocent a man might never know that under it my brother floated, dead with all his men. Lady Meredith was in sad shape, sails torn, masts splintered, nine men amongst the missing. We said prayers for the lost and set about making what repairs we could...and that afternoon, our
48	
49	crow's eye saw Sails in the distance. It was Sun Chaser, come back to find us.”
50	
51	Lady Westhill had done more than simply survive the storm. She had found land. The winds and raging seas that had separated her Sun Chaser from the Hightowers had driven her westward, and when the dawn broke, her man in the crow's nest had espied birds circling a hazy mountain peak on the horizon. Lady Alys made toward it and came upon three small islands. “A mountain attended by two hills,” as she put it. The Lady Meredith was in no fit shape to sail, but with the help of a tow from three boats off the Sun Chaser she was able to make the safety of the islands. The two battered ships sheltered off the islands for more than a fortnight, making repairs and replenishing their stores. Lady Alys was triumphant; here was land farther to the west than any land had ever been known to be, islands that existed on no known chart. Since there were three of them, she named them Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya. The islands were uninhabited, but springs and streams were plentiful, so the voyagers were able to fill their casks with all the fresh water they required. There were wild pigs as well, and huge, sluggish grey lizards as big as deer, and trees heavy with nuts and fruit.
52	
53	After sampling some of those, Eustace Hightower declared that they had no need to go any farther. “This is discovery enough,” he said. “We have spices here | have never tasted, and these pink fruits...we have our fortunes here, in our hands.”
54	
55	Alys Westhill was incredulous. Three small islands, even the largest of them a third the size of Dragonstone,
56	
57	that was nothing. The true wonders lay farther west. There might be another Essos just beyond the horizon.
58	
59	“Or there might be another thousand leagues of empty ocean,” Ser Eustace replied. And though Lady Alys cajoled and pleaded and wove webs of words in the air, she could not move him. “Even had | wished to, my crew would not allow it,” he told Lord Donnel in the Hightower. “To a man, they were convinced they had seen a giant kraken pull Autumn Moon beneath the sea. Had | given the command to sail on, they would have fed me
60	
61	to the waves and found another captain.” So the voyagers had parted ways as they left the islands. Lady Meredith turned back east for home, whilst Alys Westhill and her Sun Chaser pressed on westward, chasing the sun. Eustace Hightower's voyage home would prove to be nigh as perilous as his voyage out had been. There were more storms to be weathered, though none as terrible as the one that had claimed his brother's ship. The prevailing winds were against them, forcing them to tack and tack again. They had taken three of the great grey lizards on board, and one bit his steersman, whose leg turned green and had to be removed. A few days later, they encountered a pod of leviathans. One of them, a huge white bull larger than a ship, had slammed into Lady Meredith of a purpose, and cracked her hull. Afterward Ser Eustace had changed course, making for the Summer Islands, which he figured to be their nearest landfall. They were farther south than he had realized, however, and ended up
62	
63	missing the islands entirely and fetching up instead upon the coast of Sothoryos.
64	
65	“We were there for a full year,” he told his grandsire, “trying to make Lady Meredith seaworthy again, for the damage was greater than we'd thought. There were fortunes to be had there as well, though, and we were not blind to that. Emeralds, gold, spices, aye, all that and more. Strange creatures...monkeys that walk like men, men that howl like monkeys, wyverns, basilisks, a hundred different sorts of snakes. Deadly, all of them. Some of my men just vanished of a night. The ones who didn’t began to die. One was bitten by a fly, a little prick upon his neck, nothing to fear. Three days later his skin was sloughing off, and he was bleeding from his ears and
66	
67	cock and arse. Drinking salt water will make a man mad, every sailor knows that, but the freshwater is no safer in that place. There are worms in it, almost too small to see, if you swallowed them they laid their eggs inside you. And the fevers...hardly a day went by when half my men were fit to work. We all would have perished, | think, but some Summer Islanders passing by came on us. They know that hell better than they let on, | think.
68	
69	With their help, | was able to get Lady Meredith to Tall Trees Town, and from there to home.” There ended his tale, and his great adventure.
70	
71	As for Lady Alys Westhill, born Elissa of House Farman, where her adventure ended we cannot say. The Sun
72	
73	Chaser vanished into the west, still searching for the lands beyond the Sunset Sea, and was never seen again. Except...
74	
75	Many years later, Corlys Velaryon, the boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC, would take his ship the Sea Snake on nine great voyages, sailing farther than any man of Westeros had ever sailed before. On the first of those voyages, he sailed beyond the Jade Gates, to Yi Ti and the isle of Leng, and returned with such a wealth of spice and silk and jade that House Velaryon doubled its wealth from the profits of that single journey. On his second voyage, Ser Corlys sailed even farther east, and became the first Westerosi ever to reach Asshai-by-theShadow, the bleak black city of the shadowbinders at the edge of the world. There he lost his love and half his crew, if the tales be true...and there as well, in Asshai’s harbor, he glimpsed an old and much weathered ship
76	
77	that he would swear forevermore could only have been Sun Chaser. In 59 AC, however, Corlys Velaryon was a boy of six, dreaming of the sea, so we must leave him and turn back once again to the end of autumn in that fateful year, when the skies darkened, the winds rose, and winter
78	
79	came again to Westeros.
80	
81	The winter of 59-60 AC was an exceptionally cruel one, all those who survived it agreed. The North was hit first and hardest, as crops died in the field, streams froze, and bitter winds came howling over the Wall. Though Lord Alaric Stark had commanded that half of every harvest be preserved and put aside against the coming winter, not all his bannermen had obeyed. As their larders and granaries emptied, famine spread across the land, and old men bade farewell to their children and went out into the snow to die so their kin might live. Harvests failed in the riverlands, the westerlands, and the Vale as well, and even down into the Reach. Those who had food began to hoard, and all across the Seven Kingdoms the price of bread began to rise.
82

# The Long Reign—Jaehaerys and Alysanne: Policy, Progeny, and Pain

The Long Reign

Jachaerys and Alysanne: Policy, Progeny, and Pain n the seventh day of the 59th year after Aegon’s Conquest, a battered ship came limping up the Whispering Sound to the port of Oldtown. Her sails were patched and ragged and salt-stained, her paint faded and flaking, the banner streaming from her mast so sun-bleached as to be unrecognizable. Not until she was tied up at dock was she finally recognized in her sorry state. She was the Lady Meredith, last seen departing

Oldtown almost three years earlier to cross the Sunset Sea.

As her crew began to disembark, throngs of merchants, porters, whores, seamen, and thieves gaped in shock. Nine of every ten men coming ashore were black or brown. Ripples of excitement ran up and down the docks. Had the Lady Meredith indeed crossed the Sunset Sea? Were the peoples of the fabled lands of the far west all dark-skinned as Summer Islanders?

Only when Ser Eustace Hightower himself emerged did the whispers die. Lord Donnel’s grandson was gaunt and sun-burned, with lines on his face that had not been there when he sailed. A handful of Oldtown men were with him, all that remained of his original crew. One of his grandsire’s customs officers met him on the dock and a quick exchange ensued. The Lady Meredith's crew did not simply look like Summer Islanders; they were Summer Islanders, hired on in Sothoryos (“at ruinous wages,” Ser Eustace complained) to replace the men he'd lost. He would require porters, the captain said. His holds were bulging with rich cargo...but not from lands beyond the Sunset Sea. “That was a dream,” he said. Soon enough Lord Donnel’s knights turned up, with orders to escort him to the Hightower. There, in his grandsire's high hall with a cup of wine in hand, Ser Eustace Hightower told his tale. Lord Donnel’s scribes

scribbled as he spoke, and within days the story had spread all over Westeros, by messenger, bard, and raven.

The voyage had begun as well as he could have hoped, Ser Eustace said. Once beyond the Arbor, Lady Westhill had steered her Sun Chaser south by southwest, seeking warmer waters and fair winds, and the Lady Meredith and Autumn Moon had followed. The big Braavosi ship was very fast when the wind was in her sails, and the Hightowers had difficulties keeping pace. “The Seven were smiling on us, at the start. We had the sun by day and the moon by night, and as sweet a wind as man or maid could hope for. We were not entirely alone. We glimpsed fisherfolk from time to time, and once a great dark ship that could only have been a whaler out of Ib. And fish, so many fish...some dolphins swam beside us, as if they had never seen a ship before. We all

thought that we were blessed.”

Twelve days of smooth sailing out of Westeros, the Sun Chaser and her two companions were as far south as the Summer Islands, according to their best calculations, and farther west than any ship had sailed before... or any ship that had returned to tell of it, at least. On the Lady Meredith and Autumn Moon, casks of Arbor

gold were breached to toast the accomplishment; on Sun Chaser, the sailors drank a spiced honey wine from Lannisport. And if any man of them was disquieted that they had not seen a bird for the past four days, he held

his tongue.

The gods hate man’s arrogance, the septons teach us, and The Seven-Pointed Star says that pride goes before a fall. It may well be that Alys Westhill and the Hightowers celebrated too loudly and too early, there in the ocean deeps, for soon after that the grand voyage began to go badly wrong. “We lost the wind first,” Ser Eustace told his grandfather's court. “For almost a fortnight there was not so much as a breeze, and the ships moved only so far as we could tow them. It was discovered that a dozen casks of meat on Autumn Moon were crawling with maggots. A small enough thing by itself, but an ill omen. The wind finally returned one day near sunset, when the sky turned red as blood, but the look of it set men to muttering. | told them it boded well for

us, but | lied. Before morning the stars were gone and the wind began to howl, and then the ocean rose.”

That was the first storm, Ser Eustace said. Another followed two days later, and then a third, each worse than the one before. “The waves rose higher than our masts, and there was thunder all around, and lightning such as | had never seen before, great cracking bolts that burned the eyes. One struck the Autumn Moon and split her mast from the crow's nest down to the deck. In the midst of all that madness, one of my hands screamed that he had seen arms rising from the water, the last thing any captain needs to hear. We had lost all

sight of Sun Chaser by then, all that remained was my lady and the Moon. The sea was washing over our decks with every rise and fall, and men were being swept over the side, clinging uselessly to lines. | saw the Autumn Moon founder with my own eyes. One moment she was there, broken and burning, but there. Then a wave rose up and swallowed her and | blinked and she was gone, quick as that. That was all it was, a wave, a monster of a

wave, but all my men were screaming ‘Kraken, kraken!’ and not a word | said would ever disabuse them.

“| will never know how we survived that night, but we did. The next morning the sea was calm again, the sun was shining, and the water was so blue and innocent a man might never know that under it my brother floated, dead with all his men. Lady Meredith was in sad shape, sails torn, masts splintered, nine men amongst the missing. We said prayers for the lost and set about making what repairs we could...and that afternoon, our

crow's eye saw Sails in the distance. It was Sun Chaser, come back to find us.”

Lady Westhill had done more than simply survive the storm. She had found land. The winds and raging seas that had separated her Sun Chaser from the Hightowers had driven her westward, and when the dawn broke, her man in the crow's nest had espied birds circling a hazy mountain peak on the horizon. Lady Alys made toward it and came upon three small islands. “A mountain attended by two hills,” as she put it. The Lady Meredith was in no fit shape to sail, but with the help of a tow from three boats off the Sun Chaser she was able to make the safety of the islands. The two battered ships sheltered off the islands for more than a fortnight, making repairs and replenishing their stores. Lady Alys was triumphant; here was land farther to the west than any land had ever been known to be, islands that existed on no known chart. Since there were three of them, she named them Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya. The islands were uninhabited, but springs and streams were plentiful, so the voyagers were able to fill their casks with all the fresh water they required. There were wild pigs as well, and huge, sluggish grey lizards as big as deer, and trees heavy with nuts and fruit.

After sampling some of those, Eustace Hightower declared that they had no need to go any farther. “This is discovery enough,” he said. “We have spices here | have never tasted, and these pink fruits...we have our fortunes here, in our hands.”

Alys Westhill was incredulous. Three small islands, even the largest of them a third the size of Dragonstone,

that was nothing. The true wonders lay farther west. There might be another Essos just beyond the horizon.

“Or there might be another thousand leagues of empty ocean,” Ser Eustace replied. And though Lady Alys cajoled and pleaded and wove webs of words in the air, she could not move him. “Even had | wished to, my crew would not allow it,” he told Lord Donnel in the Hightower. “To a man, they were convinced they had seen a giant kraken pull Autumn Moon beneath the sea. Had | given the command to sail on, they would have fed me

to the waves and found another captain.” So the voyagers had parted ways as they left the islands. Lady Meredith turned back east for home, whilst Alys Westhill and her Sun Chaser pressed on westward, chasing the sun. Eustace Hightower's voyage home would prove to be nigh as perilous as his voyage out had been. There were more storms to be weathered, though none as terrible as the one that had claimed his brother's ship. The prevailing winds were against them, forcing them to tack and tack again. They had taken three of the great grey lizards on board, and one bit his steersman, whose leg turned green and had to be removed. A few days later, they encountered a pod of leviathans. One of them, a huge white bull larger than a ship, had slammed into Lady Meredith of a purpose, and cracked her hull. Afterward Ser Eustace had changed course, making for the Summer Islands, which he figured to be their nearest landfall. They were farther south than he had realized, however, and ended up

missing the islands entirely and fetching up instead upon the coast of Sothoryos.

“We were there for a full year,” he told his grandsire, “trying to make Lady Meredith seaworthy again, for the damage was greater than we'd thought. There were fortunes to be had there as well, though, and we were not blind to that. Emeralds, gold, spices, aye, all that and more. Strange creatures...monkeys that walk like men, men that howl like monkeys, wyverns, basilisks, a hundred different sorts of snakes. Deadly, all of them. Some of my men just vanished of a night. The ones who didn’t began to die. One was bitten by a fly, a little prick upon his neck, nothing to fear. Three days later his skin was sloughing off, and he was bleeding from his ears and

cock and arse. Drinking salt water will make a man mad, every sailor knows that, but the freshwater is no safer in that place. There are worms in it, almost too small to see, if you swallowed them they laid their eggs inside you. And the fevers...hardly a day went by when half my men were fit to work. We all would have perished, | think, but some Summer Islanders passing by came on us. They know that hell better than they let on, | think.

With their help, | was able to get Lady Meredith to Tall Trees Town, and from there to home.” There ended his tale, and his great adventure.

As for Lady Alys Westhill, born Elissa of House Farman, where her adventure ended we cannot say. The Sun

Chaser vanished into the west, still searching for the lands beyond the Sunset Sea, and was never seen again. Except...

Many years later, Corlys Velaryon, the boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC, would take his ship the Sea Snake on nine great voyages, sailing farther than any man of Westeros had ever sailed before. On the first of those voyages, he sailed beyond the Jade Gates, to Yi Ti and the isle of Leng, and returned with such a wealth of spice and silk and jade that House Velaryon doubled its wealth from the profits of that single journey. On his second voyage, Ser Corlys sailed even farther east, and became the first Westerosi ever to reach Asshai-by-theShadow, the bleak black city of the shadowbinders at the edge of the world. There he lost his love and half his crew, if the tales be true...and there as well, in Asshai’s harbor, he glimpsed an old and much weathered ship

that he would swear forevermore could only have been Sun Chaser. In 59 AC, however, Corlys Velaryon was a boy of six, dreaming of the sea, so we must leave him and turn back once again to the end of autumn in that fateful year, when the skies darkened, the winds rose, and winter

came again to Westeros.

The winter of 59-60 AC was an exceptionally cruel one, all those who survived it agreed. The North was hit first and hardest, as crops died in the field, streams froze, and bitter winds came howling over the Wall. Though Lord Alaric Stark had commanded that half of every harvest be preserved and put aside against the coming winter, not all his bannermen had obeyed. As their larders and granaries emptied, famine spread across the land, and old men bade farewell to their children and went out into the snow to die so their kin might live. Harvests failed in the riverlands, the westerlands, and the Vale as well, and even down into the Reach. Those who had food began to hoard, and all across the Seven Kingdoms the price of bread began to rise.

input.content:
# FAB — The Long Reign—Jaehaerys and Alysanne: Policy, Progeny, and Pain (part whole)

## Entity Roster
| Name (as in book) | Type guess | Disambiguator (from THIS text only) | New-to-me? | Canonical-spelling note (if OCR-garbled) |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------------------------------|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| Eustace Hightower | person | Ser; grandson of Lord Donnel Hightower; captain of the Lady Meredith | unknown | — |
| Donnel Hightower | person | Lord of the Hightower (Oldtown); grandsire of Ser Eustace | unknown | text has bare "Lord Donnel"; canonical Donnel Hightower |
| Elissa Farman | person | born Elissa of House Farman; captain of the Sun Chaser | unknown | — |
| Alys Westhill | person | assumed name of Elissa Farman; "Lady Alys Westhill"/"Lady Westhill" | unknown | — |
| Corlys Velaryon | person | boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC; later captain of the Sea Snake | unknown | — |
| Alaric Stark | person | Lord (of the North); commanded harvest be preserved for winter | unknown | — |
| Brother of Eustace Hightower | person | unnamed; captained the Autumn Moon; drowned when she foundered | unknown | no name given in text |
| Lady Meredith | ship | Hightower ship captained by Ser Eustace | unknown | — |
| Sun Chaser | ship | fast Braavosi ship captained by Alys Westhill (Elissa Farman) | unknown | — |
| Autumn Moon | ship | Hightower ship captained by Eustace's brother; foundered | unknown | — |
| Sea Snake | ship | Corlys Velaryon's ship of nine great voyages | unknown | — |
| House Farman | house | house Elissa was born into | unknown | — |
| House Velaryon | house | Corlys Velaryon's house; wealth doubled by his first voyage | unknown | — |
| House Hightower | house | "the Hightowers" of Oldtown | unknown | — |
| Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya (islands) | place | three western islands named by Alys Westhill | unknown | — |
| Oldtown | place | port on the Whispering Sound | unknown | — |
| Whispering Sound | place | approach to Oldtown | unknown | — |
| Sunset Sea | place | ocean west of Westeros crossed by the expedition | unknown | — |
| Arbor | place | passed at voyage's start | unknown | — |
| Summer Islands | place | southern islands; Summer Islanders hired there | unknown | — |
| Sothoryos | place | southern continent where Lady Meredith was stranded a year | unknown | — |
| Tall Trees Town | place | Summer Islands port Eustace reached | unknown | — |
| Jade Gates | place | passage Corlys sailed beyond on his first voyage | unknown | — |
| Yi Ti | place | eastern land Corlys reached | unknown | — |
| Leng | place | isle Corlys reached | unknown | — |
| Asshai-by-the-Shadow | place | eastern city Corlys first reached; "Asshai-by-theShadow" | unknown | OCR: "Asshai-by-theShadow" (missing hyphen/space) |
| Ib | place | origin of a whaler glimpsed at sea | unknown | — |
| Lannisport | place | source of spiced honey wine | unknown | — |
| Driftmark | place | Corlys Velaryon's birthplace | unknown | — |
| Dragonstone | place | used as a size comparison for the islands | unknown | — |
| the Wall | place | winter winds came howling over it | unknown | — |
| the North | place | hit first and hardest by the winter | unknown | — |
| riverlands | place | harvests failed there | unknown | — |
| westerlands | place | harvests failed there | unknown | — |
| Vale | place | harvests failed there | unknown | — |
| Reach | place | harvests failed "even down into the Reach" | unknown | — |
| Seven Kingdoms | place | price of bread rose across it | unknown | — |
| Westeros | place | homeland the voyagers sailed from | unknown | — |
| Essos | place | "another Essos just beyond the horizon" | unknown | — |
| The Seven-Pointed Star | reference | scripture cited: "pride goes before a fall" | unknown | — |
| the Seven | reference | "The Seven were smiling on us" | unknown | — |
| Aegon's Conquest | event | dating epoch: "59th year after Aegon's Conquest" | unknown | — |

## Node Prose
### Eustace Hightower
Ser Eustace Hightower was the grandson of Lord Donnel Hightower and captained the Lady Meredith on the western voyage out of Oldtown — quote: "Lord Donnel’s grandson". His ship returned to Oldtown battered after nearly three years, her crew mostly Summer Islanders hired in Sothoryos — quote: "hired on in Sothoryos". His brother captained the accompanying Autumn Moon and drowned when she foundered in a storm — quote: "under it my brother floated, dead with all his men". He refused to sail farther west and turned back at the newfound islands, then was stranded on Sothoryos for a year before reaching home — quote: "We were there for a full year".

### Elissa Farman
Born Elissa of House Farman, she is called Lady Alys Westhill and Lady Westhill in this account — quote: "born Elissa of House Farman". She captained the fast Braavosi ship Sun Chaser, seeking the lands beyond the Sunset Sea — quote: "steered her Sun Chaser south by southwest". She discovered three uninhabited western islands and named them — quote: "she named them Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya". When Eustace turned back she pressed on westward, and the Sun Chaser was never seen again — quote: "vanished into the west, still searching for the lands beyond the Sunset Sea".

### Corlys Velaryon
Corlys Velaryon was a boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC, a boy of six in 59 AC — quote: "the boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC". He would later sail nine great voyages aboard the Sea Snake, reaching Yi Ti, Leng, and Asshai — quote: "take his ship the Sea Snake on nine great voyages". He became the first Westerosi to reach Asshai, and House Velaryon doubled its wealth from his first voyage — quote: "became the first Westerosi ever to reach Asshai-by-theShadow". In Asshai's harbor he glimpsed a ship he swore was the Sun Chaser, though the account is hedged — quote: "if the tales be true".

### Lady Meredith
The Lady Meredith was a Hightower ship that limped back into Oldtown in 59 AC after almost three years at sea — quote: "last seen departing". A leviathan cracked her hull, forcing Eustace to make for Sothoryos — quote: "slammed into Lady Meredith of a purpose, and cracked her hull".

### Sun Chaser
The Sun Chaser was a fast Braavosi ship captained by Alys Westhill — quote: "The big Braavosi ship was very fast". Driven west by the storm, she found land unknown to any chart, then sailed on and vanished — quote: "She had found land".

### Autumn Moon
The Autumn Moon was a Hightower ship captained by Eustace's brother — quote: "the Lady Meredith and Autumn Moon had followed". She foundered in a great storm with all her men lost — quote: "saw the Autumn Moon founder with my own eyes".

### Donnel Hightower
Lord Donnel was Eustace's grandsire, in whose high hall the tale was told and recorded by his scribes — quote: "in his grandsire's high hall".

### Alaric Stark
Lord Alaric Stark had commanded that half of every harvest be preserved against the coming winter, though not all his bannermen obeyed — quote: "Lord Alaric Stark had commanded that half of every harvest be preserved".

## Relationships Observed
| Source (name) | EDGE_TYPE [(qualifier)] | Target (name) | in_universe_source | disputed | evidence_quote (verbatim) |
|---------------|-------------------------|---------------|--------------------|----------|---------------------------|
| Donnel Hightower | ANCESTOR_OF | Eustace Hightower | | | Lord Donnel’s grandson |
| Elissa Farman | SAME_AS | Alys Westhill | | | born Elissa of House Farman |
| Elissa Farman | MEMBER_OF | House Farman | | | born Elissa of House Farman |
| Eustace Hightower | MEMBER_OF | House Hightower | | | the Hightowers had difficulties keeping pace |
| Eustace Hightower | SIBLING_OF (full) | Brother of Eustace Hightower | | | under it my brother floated, dead with all his men |
| Corlys Velaryon | MEMBER_OF | House Velaryon | | | House Velaryon doubled its wealth |

## Events of Note
| Event (name) | type (event.*) | year (AC) | agent | patient | instrument | location | outcome | in_universe_source | disputed | quote (verbatim) |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|-------|---------|------------|----------|---------|--------------------|----------|------------------|
| Western voyage of the Sun Chaser | event.voyage | | Elissa Farman | | Sun Chaser | Sunset Sea | expedition sailed farther west than any returning ship | | | Oldtown almost three years earlier to cross the Sunset Sea |
| Founder of the Autumn Moon | event.shipwreck | | | Brother of Eustace Hightower | storm-wave (crew claimed a kraken) | Sunset Sea | ship foundered; Eustace's brother and all his men drowned | unattributed | true | convinced they had seen a giant kraken pull Autumn Moon beneath the sea |
| Discovery of three western islands | event.discovery | | Elissa Farman | | | Sunset Sea | three uninhabited islands found and named Aegon, Rhaenys, Visenya | | | she named them Aegon, Rhaenys, and Visenya |
| Disappearance of the Sun Chaser | event.disappearance | | Elissa Farman | Sun Chaser | | Sunset Sea | Sun Chaser sailed on west and was never seen again | | | vanished into the west, still searching for the lands beyond the Sunset Sea |
| Corlys Velaryon reaches Asshai | event.voyage | | Corlys Velaryon | | Sea Snake | Asshai-by-the-Shadow | first Westerosi to reach Asshai; lost his love and half his crew | | | became the first Westerosi ever to reach Asshai-by-theShadow |
| Sun Chaser glimpsed in Asshai's harbor | event.sighting | | Corlys Velaryon | Sun Chaser | | Asshai-by-the-Shadow | ship sworn to be Sun Chaser seen years later | unattributed | true | that he would swear forevermore could only have been Sun Chaser |
| Winter of 59-60 AC | event.famine | 59 | | | | Seven Kingdoms | cruel winter; crops died, famine spread, bread prices rose | | | The winter of 59-60 AC was an exceptionally cruel one |

## Harvest sidecar
FAB / Oldtown almost three years earlier to cross the Sunset Sea / targaryen-history / Sun Chaser expedition sets out to cross the Sunset Sea
FAB / born Elissa of House Farman / cross-identity / Alys Westhill is Elissa Farman
FAB / that he would swear forevermore could only have been Sun Chaser / foreshadow-hook / Corlys glimpses a ship he swears is Sun Chaser at Asshai (hedged)
FAB / The winter of 59-60 AC was an exceptionally cruel one / targaryen-history / severe winter strikes Westeros
FAB / crops died in the field / causal-spine / cruel winter → crops die → famine → bread prices rise

content.text:
Proposal written to `/Users/mnoth/source/asoiaf-chat/extractions/fire-and-blood/fab-the-long-reign-13.enrichment.md`, with 5 breadcrumbs appended to the harvest sidecar.

This unit isn't about court policy despite the section title — it's the tale of the failed western expedition (Sun Chaser / Lady Meredith / Autumn Moon) plus the cruel winter of 59-60 AC. What I captured:

- **Roster** — the voyage's principals (Ser Eustace Hightower, Elissa Farman/Alys Westhill, Corlys Velaryon, Lord Donnel Hightower, Lord Alaric Stark, Eustace's unnamed drowned brother), the four ships as entities, and the geography/houses named in-text. Disambiguators drawn only from this unit (e.g. Corlys as "boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC").
- **Node prose** — full blocks for the voyage principals and ships; one-liners for Donnel and Alaric Stark.
- **Relationships** — Donnel ANCESTOR_OF Eustace, the in-text identity reveal Elissa Farman `SAME_AS` Alys Westhill, house memberships, and Eustace `SIBLING_OF (full)` his unnamed brother.
- **Events** — the western voyage, the Autumn Moon foundering, island discovery, Sun Chaser's disappearance, Corlys reaching Asshai, and the winter/famine.

Two provenance flags set (`disputed: true`, `unattributed`): the kraken-vs-wave cause of the Autumn Moon's loss, and the hedged "if the tales be true" sighting of Sun Chaser in Asshai's harbor. Everything else is plain Gyldayn narration, left blank for Tier 1. All quotes copied verbatim including OCR garbles (e.g. `Asshai-by-theShadow`).
